articles.title: Tesla's lithium refinery discharges 231,000 gallons of polluted wastewater a day
articles.summary: Tesla's lithium refinery discharges 231,000 gallons of polluted wastewater a day, causing significant environmental concerns for developers and electric vehicle manufacturers. The drainage district in Nueces County, Texas, found the pipe they were monitoring to be discharging black liquid into an unnamed ditch that flows into Petronila Creek and from there into Baffin Bay. The drainage district had not been informed about Tesla's wastewater discharge permit and did not grant Tesla the right to use public or private property for wastewater conveyance. This discovery has led to a growing concern among developers and electric vehicle manufacturers about the environmental impact of Tesla's lithium refinery operations.

articles.title: Dumb Ways for an Open Source Project to Die
articles.summary: Weekend at Bernie’s showed that a good chunk of the most-depended-on open source packages are dead, with various ways for a project to end up that way. The simplest and most common case: last human commit some years back, issues accumulating unanswered, the repo not archived so it doesn’t show up in any filter that would flag it. Usually the maintainer just moved on to other things and the project wasn't important enough to them to formally hand over or shut down, though the same silence covers everything up to and including the maintainer having died, which neither the registry nor the repo has any way to represent. From outside it’s indistinguishable from a long holiday until enough unanswered issues have piled up to make the silence unambiguous, and the npm utilities at the top of the Bernie’s dead list are mostly this. Corporate orphan.

articles.title: Pokemon compression myth
articles.summary: In this article, we explore the core topic of Pokémon compression, which is a technique used by developers to reduce the size of large datasets. The key technology behind Pokémon compression is Huffman coding, a method that assigns shorter codes to more frequent characters in a text. This reduces the amount of memory needed to store and process the data, making it easier for developers to work with larger datasets. The article also discusses how Pokémon compression can improve performance by reducing the number of operations required to manipulate large datasets. However, it's important to note that while Pokémon compression is useful, it may not be suitable for all use cases due to its limitations in handling certain types of data and edge cases.

articles.title: Type out the code
articles.summary:
Type out the code

Back in the day I read one of Zed Shaw's "Learn X the hard way" courses (I think it was his C++ course) and he began the course with a disclaimer that said something like "do not copy and paste the examples; type them out" because he believed strongly that doing so improved your comprehension of the material. These days, he recommends something even stronger: The best way to do this is when you complete an exercise delete what you did and do it again, but try to use your memory. If you get stuck then look at the exercise for clues, but try your best to recreate what you just did from memory. At first you'll be constantly referring to the exercise and the code, but eventually you'll get to where you can do it on your own. This will also improve your ability to remember more code, your ability to summarize how something works, and generally improve your skills faster than if you did it once and moved on.

articles.title: The Super Tiny Compiler, but in Ada
articles.summary:
The Super Tiny Compiler, a lightweight Ada compiler, is designed to be small and efficient. It's built on the principles of Ada, making it suitable for both beginners and experienced developers alike. The key technology behind this compiler is its ability to handle simple arithmetic operations in a single line using "add" and "subtract". This makes it ideal for projects where performance is critical, such as in educational or scientific applications.

The Super Tiny Compiler's core functionality lies within the `stcc` file, which contains the main code. It can be used with both Tada (a tool that compiles Ada source code into C) and other compilers like GCC. The compiler supports a wide range of features, including type inference, variable scoping, and automatic typing.

The Super Tiny Compiler's use in Ada is particularly relevant because it aligns well with the language's design principles, which emphasize simplicity and efficiency.

articles.title: Software's Centaur Era
articles.summary: Software's Centaur Era by Richard Marmorstein - May 18, 2026 ← Home t’s 2030. I wake up, make my coffee, drag myself over to my laptop. Then I remember – AI has completely replaced knowledge work. I open up my agent chat, type “still got access to my bank account? Please continue making money.” I hesitate. Should I add something else? Nudge the AI toward a problem area I personally judge to be important or interesting? I resist, for as that would constitute knowledge work (which AI has entirely replaced), it would strictly decrease my expected return. I scratch that itch instead by starting Wordle with something other than SLATE. I solve in 5, finish my coffee, and then get to work at my side gig: driving around doing TaskRabbit jobs at the behest of AIs that are not (yet) otherwise capable of influencing the physical world.

articles.title: Growing Neural Cellular Automata
articles.summary:
Growing Neural Cellular Automata: Differentiable Model of Morphogenesis

The article explores how differentiable models of morphogenesis are being developed to simulate complex biological processes. These models can generate patterns but lack the ability to persist them, making them useful for training neural networks. The authors discuss three types of persistent models—those that make patterns stay for a prolonged period and those that develop regenerative capabilities without explicit instructions.

The article highlights how morphogenesis is one of the most fundamental skills every living creature possesses. Understanding the interplay between simple rules and homeostatic feedback loops can help researchers in fields like computer science, mathematics, and biology. The development of differentiable models of morphogenesis could lead to more efficient training of neural networks, potentially improving their performance on tasks such as image recognition.

The article emphasizes that while these models are still in the experimental phase, they offer a promising approach for understanding complex biological processes.
